facts_disclosed: Equine cert dead — too expensive, doesn't like horses

turns.content: you know what. remember I was looking at doing extra qualifications. equine surgery cert. forget I said anything. its dead.
Oh, the idea's off the table? What happened -- was it a cost thing, or just didn't feel right?

both tbh. looked at what the course actually costs and its absolutely mental. were talking thousands for a six month programme and thats before you factor in travel and materials and all the rest of it.
Yeah, those specialist certifications can be eye-wateringly expensive. Was it going to be worth it in terms of what you'd earn back, or was the return on investment not there either?

prob would have paid for itself eventually with the horse work up here but ngl I had a bit of a moment of honesty with myself. I dont even really like horses. massive dramatic creatures. everything is a crisis with them. one wee scratch and theyre acting like theyve been shot.
Ha, that's a pretty solid reason to not pursue a whole career path working with them. If the animals themselves wind you up, no amount of good money is going to make that enjoyable day to day.

exactly. cattle are straightforward. they either have a problem or they dont. theres no performance about it. a cow doesnt make eye contact with you and tremble because theres a puddle. horses are just enormous toddlers with hooves.
"Enormous toddlers with hooves" is a very vivid description. Sounds like you've made peace with the decision pretty quickly though -- no regrets?
zero. not even a wee bit. ill stick with cattle and sheep. they dont need me to like them and I dont need to pretend I enjoy being around them. its a professional relationship. works grand.
There's something to be said for knowing exactly what you want to do and not chasing qualifications you won't enjoy using. Saves you a lot of money and misery.

aye exactly. sometimes the smartest thing you can do is just not bother. the whole idea was a bad one from the start I think. got a bit carried away by the money side of it and forgot that id actually have to spend time with horses.
That's a pretty common trap -- seeing the earning potential and overlooking the daily reality of the work. At least you figured it out before you were halfway through the course and £3,000 lighter.
aye that would have been worse. much worse. sitting in a lecture about equine anatomy thinking what am I doing here. nah. its off the table completely. moving on.
Clean break. Fair enough. At least now you can put that time and energy into what you actually enjoy -- or at least professionally tolerate. Cattle and sheep aren't going anywhere.
they are not. theres always more cattle. theres always more sheep. job security through sheer volume of livestock. cant argue with that.
Reliable demand is underrated. And at least your patients don't throw tantrums over puddles. Sounds like the right call.
